does: SAGE-RT introduces a taxonomy-driven pipeline for automatically generating synthetic alignment and red-teaming datasets at scale without requiring extensive manual seed data. The method produces 51,000 diverse prompt-response pairs covering over 1,500 topics of potential harmfulness and various jailbreaking techniques. The pipeline systematically generates nuanced variations across a multi-level taxonomy structure including high-level categories, sub-categories, and fine-grained leaf-categories. The authors demonstrate that red-teaming prompts generated through SAGE achieve 100% attack success rates against GPT-4o and GPT-3.5-turbo on multiple sub-categories of harmfulness, and successfully jailbreak these state-of-the-art models across the majority of tested categories, establishing the quality and effectiveness of their synthetic data.
connects: This work builds on the growing need for scalable red-teaming and adversarial evaluation methods for large language models, extending prior approaches that relied on manual annotation or limited seed data. It addresses recognized limitations in existing synthetic data generation methods that struggle with nuance, diversity, and controllability. SAGE combines insights from taxonomy-based data organization with automated prompt generation techniques, responding to the challenge that manual red-teaming becomes infeasible as models scale. The work relates to prior efforts in jailbreak research, safety benchmarking, and adversarial prompt generation, while improving upon them by providing systematic coverage of harm categories and concrete validation against production models.
enables: SAGE-RT enables more efficient safety evaluation workflows by providing researchers and practitioners with large-scale, diverse red-teaming datasets that can be continuously generated and refined. The taxonomy-driven approach allows for systematic exploration of new harmful categories and jailbreaking strategies, supporting future development of more robust safety measures. The demonstrated attack success rates suggest this synthetic data could be used for adversarial training and fine-tuning of safer models. The scalable pipeline enables real-time adaptation to emerging jailbreaking techniques and allows deployment teams to proactively evaluate model vulnerabilities. This work points toward automated, interpretable safety evaluation frameworks and could facilitate the development of more comprehensive safety standards across the LLM industry.
